sentences.text: OK.
You see, we were so completely isolated.
There was no source in the ghetto of obtaining arms to fight to resist physically.
So the-- one of the first assignments was to gather ammunition.
The only place to gather it, to find it, was outside the ghetto.
Either by donations from any group, any organization outside the ghetto that would support it, or by buying it, paying for it.
It was like a street.
The partisans knew, they had a map in their heads, or maybe even drew them for all I know.
So they were able to move within a square block.
The blocks were quite long, within a cube actually.
Square blocks all around.
When they had to go from one block to the other and they could not-- if they could not do it on the streets, they used sewers.
And to get in, they knew every sewer that was, you know, one-- one assignment.
They knew every sewer in-- in the ghetto.
And they-- and through the sewers, they would get into-- into to the Polish side, and then they brought back the-- the ammunition.
And then they-- then-- then the-- then more and more confrontations occurred.
Sometimes, you see, I think they were more valiant than the Maccabees.
They were just-- they were just a handful of them.
Well, very few because there were very few left.
And they really had to use ruse.
They had-- they had to-- they had to use a lot of ingenuity in addition to all the courage.
And they often would lure them in, the Germans.
And-- and-- and there were confrontations.
Sometimes they were provoked if-- if-- if-- if that was in the plans of the partisans.
Sometimes they would provoke.
Sometimes they would appear, the Germans would appear, and it was a response.
Sometimes it was a response to the Germans and sometimes it was provoked by the-- by the partisans.
Now, I think that the partisans wanted in some way to send out a message to the Germans.
It's not so safe to come in anymore into that little enclave that was left.
So I guess at that point they invited sometimes confrontations and they-- they-- they used a ruse.
They-- they would lure them in.
Now, up until then the Germans, why, there was still a certain amount of fear of being attacked, because they were sporadic times.
But, you know, they were very-- they were very unapproachable.

Because even I remember having the fantasy-- and my sister, and my mother, we shared it-- we wanted a job, but you knew that there's always the gun between-- between you and that physical person that you wanted to attack.
So you can always be blown away, and there-- there-- there is-- there is no resistance in that.
So-- so-- so the Germans did have a certain fear.
Now at this point, when these confrontations occurred, they were much more cautious.
They could not-- they were not total-- totally in control.
They could not go in-- into the ghetto, step in-- into the ghetto.
You know, the buildings were not dense.
It was not that every apartment was occupied.
There were only-- you know, you could be shot.
They had the freedom, the-- the-- the partisans, the-- the run of the whole building.
And if they would go into-- if the Germans would go into one-- so many apartments would be empty.
They never knew where one was lurking.
They sometimes would lure them up to-- to the attics.
They might start from a lower level, but lure them up to the attics.
There the Germans were completely lost because the partisans knew their way.
This-- this was their territory.
So they tried to lure them into their territories.
Relief.
I'm sure that there was also mixed with a certain fear.
I'm sure that-- I am sure that even the-- freedo-- the resistance fighters must have asked themselves.
You know, the peril.
It was not just your own-- it-- when-- when you-- when you volunteer yourself, you volunteer your own life.
But you know, the reprisals were-- were horrendous.
Yes.
Yes.
And please don't let me forget to tell you about another role model that was very important to me, Mr.
Szabatnik.
Yes.
My cousin Moniek was a partisan.
And he came once when we were already-- we lived at that time on a ground floor, and we had a bunker below-- below us.
And the way we entered the bunker was we had a powder room.
Excuse me.
And the whole floor of the powder room lifted up, so that you couldn't see any trapdoor.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I forgot what I was telling you.
Oh, about the two partisans that I had close contact with.
And this-- my cousin Moniek came to visit.
He-- he just appeared, told us that, you know, his whole family was gone.
And we knew everyone else in our family was gone, and he was the first contact outside our immediate family that we had.
I remember him coming and telling us about-- he-- he was in a group on [?
Mewa ?] Street.
I guess this is he was in the headquarters, I think.
And-- and he-- and he was telling us about-- we were just-- you know, the heroic things, how they got the ammunition.
And he was just so-- so committed.
Then there was another person, a young boy, his name was Benio Posner, and he was a very close friend of ours.
His whole family vanished.
But then one day-- you know, the-- the partisans moved around.
They were-- they were like-- like-- they would-- like magic little creatures.
They would-- you would see them here.
You'd see them there, and they would disappear.
And of course there was a great deal of pride and love we felt for them.
We felt like they are-- that they are shining for us.
And-- he-- Benio came and he told us too that his whole family was gone.
And he had some kind of fracture with-- on his-- on his-- either his foot, his ankle, or his toe was either fractured or sprained.
I don't remember.
But I remember that when he left, we were very, very concerned about him.
Miraculously, after the war, we found out that he survived and that he-- he-- he went to a friend's.
And he was-- he attended the Sorbonne, and that a professor used some anti-Semitic expression.
And he couldn't contain himself.
He stood up.
He slapped the professor.
He said, I can't take it anymore.
He left friends and went to Israel.
And he-- he was a pilot and I-- he's still alive, and he's fine.
Moniek, we never heard from him anymore.
That was the end.
And now Mr.
Szabatnik.
One day-- now, the-- the-- the bunkers were-- were there, and they were ready.
But we did not live in them because we were still working.
But this was-- we knew that our existence was temporary.
I mean, our legal existence was temporary.
So this was reserved for when-- when all the last few streets in Warsaw are cleaned of-- of Jews.
And one morning the door opens up and two SS men walk in.
It was early in the morning.
We thought that was the end.
And Mr.
Szabatnik, he-- he was with us.
He was in the bunker with us.
I mean, in-- when I say he was in the bunker, he was a member of those, because the bunker was shared with a larger group.
I think that most of his family either were hiding somewhere else or-- or they-- they might have already been killed.
The Germans singled him out, and they took him with-- with him.
And after they left, we quickly jumped into the bunkers.
They took Mr.
Szabatnik, and they asked him-- they-- they made him go from apartment to apartment.
See, they were afraid to-- to barge into an apartment.
And they told him to knock on the door and to say to the people, open the door.
It's safe.
It's a Jew.
Well, he told them either in Polish or in Yiddish don't open the door, because there are two Germans.
Save yourselves.
Don't-- hide.
And, well, the Germans kind of sensed there must be some-- because all they got from all the knocking on the doors was silence.
And they knew that-- that-- that there are more Jews there.
So they started to beat him.
And they beat his head to a pulp.
He never, ever ceased to say the same thing.
He came, he was beyond recognition.
His-- his blood was oozing from his eyes, from this ears, from his nose.
He was all chopped up.
And yet, he felt good.
So that's where my role models came from.
Yes.
Yes.
OK.
Now, when-- when the partisans made themselves more known, the-- the Germans increased their-- their-- their cruelty, their attacks.
And they-- they-- they-- there were more frequent confrontations.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I forgot what you asked me.
Right.
Now, at the same time, they-- they saved some-- there was one particular little boy that we saw who was roaming-- who was roaming in the basements, because people were starting to build bunkers.
And so you-- you could see him roaming underground, and he would disappear.
Well, we knew who he was, but what can you do?
We kind of resist, which is we can't kill a child.
We never-- we never-- and-- so there were some people, a few people, and they didn't need many, who apparently communicated to the-- to the Germans where the bunkers were.
Now, I am not sure that every bunker that was broken into, that was-- that was ripped open, was because they knew where they were going, the Germans.
You see, what happened was that the Germans intensified their vigilance.
They start-- they snuffed out every Jewish life, every partisan.
And so they became even more violent.
So for all I know, you know, it was a basement, you just threw a grenade.
However, I think that they threw-- when they entered our bunker, they threw the grenade in the precise spot where the entrance was.
But I do remember-- well, then-- then you had these constant attacks, and they try to snuff out all the life, and then we went into the bunkers.
Then-- then our existence was completely subterraneal.
I remember that when we came into our bunkers, there were the partisans.
We had no idea that they were meeting there.
There was a meeting place.
And I think it was the [?
Hoffman ?] Shop, I think they called them.
But, you know, I-- I-- I don't remember whether the names are correct or not, but there they were.
Well, my father shared so much with us.
He did not-- we had no idea.
I don't know if my mother even knew about it, but there they were.
Yes?
When you went underground--
Yes?
It was-- see-- OK.
At that time, if you would walk into the Warsaw ghetto, you would see an awful lot of emptiness, a haunting emptiness, and a haunting quietness.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] It was a no man's land.
There-- there were many buildings that were demolished.
There were many buildings that were burned out, but empty, staring emptiness.
But the building that we were in was not touched yet.
Now, I know what we used for fuel.
We would-- throughout that time, there was no way for us to obtain anything, really, outside.
There was no-- any supplies for existence.
But for fuels, we used-- we used to call it [NON-ENGLISH].
We would go into-- we used furniture from the buildings.
Furniture.
Doors.
My parents were so proud of me, how well I used the ax to chop, to break a door down for fuel.
So we used that.
So, yes.

We were-- and really, a lot of our existence just to-- to survive, our survival depended on the resources in-- in that building as meager and as-- there was-- and as meaningless as it was, but some, like wood furniture, that we could use for fuel was-- was very important.
What did you eat?
There was very little to eat, very little.
But, you see, everything is relative.
If you ask me what did you eat, I don't remember, and it doesn't matter because there were other things at that point that bothered me much more.
But certainly, there wasn't enough food.
We had some food in the bunkers.
The bunkers were very, very well-equipped.
We had-- we had battery-run lights.
We had battery-run-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I think-- a lot-- I don't recall all the details.
But there was provision made for water.
I don't even remember how.
And I don't remember how real-- it was probably like building a bunker so that-- for the atom bomb, to protect yourself from the atom bomb.
Probably a lot of the preparation was more hope than-- than in reality, it would suitable for survival.
But-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So-- so-- so what was happening was-- so we were in the bunkers and finally-- now, we were for a very short time, that was April 1943.
And this was the full-fledged uprising where things came to a head, where the Germans decided we're going to snuff every bit of life out.
And the last I remember was that a grenade was thrown into that opening and there the Germans came down.
I remember the explosion.
I remember the panic.
I lost my voice.
My-- my-- my throat kind of swell up.
And we were dragged out.
And at that time, everything around us was-- was absolute hell.
It was absolute Valhalla.
Every building, every-- it was-- everything was detonated around us.
The-- the flames-- the flames and the smoke went-- touched the heavens.
We walked over-- over the crumble.
We-- our-- we-- we watched our-- our buildings feeding the flames.
And they took us then to umschlagplatz.
And they loaded us into the freight trains, box trains.
I don't know if they were cattle trains, but they certainly looked like it.
And at this umschlagplatz, you had this little remainder of-- of-- of the community in such fright.

And you know, I-- I remember when we walked into the-- when they-- when they pushed us, and-- and of course whips, and-- and-- and insults, and slaps, and kicks, and-- and shouts, [SPEAKING GERMAN] which means waste.
And we were pushed into the freight cars, and I remember looking out of the freight cars, the-- the the sounds, the-- the sights, and-- and-- and the flames were just so enormous.
It-- it was just-- it was-- and-- there was a-- a prism of-- of hues, of-- of the fires that went all the way up from the ground and just into the sky.
In a way, it-- it-- it was-- it was not like-- like reality.
I don't-- I don't think you could capture it on a movie, these flames licking the skies, and-- and the smoke, every-- every hue, every range of color.
And there they shut us into-- into-- into the freight cars very dense, choking.
Our lips were parched.
And-- and-- and they were shooting, shooting into the trains.
I remember how we clung to my mother and father.
The three of us clung to one another.
And those who survived the shooting into the cars were dumped off in Majdanek.
And-- and-- and all that was left of Warsaw was a big graveyard.
That's it.
A smoldering graveyard.
To Majdanek.
[SIGHS] We were exhausted.
In the train, there was blood.
There were people wounded, the people screaming, people sitting in absolute silence, actually.
And then when we arrived in Majdanek-- and we knew of Majdanek by then.
Actually, Majdanek was like Treblinka.
It was in-- in Lublin.
It was a little farther away from Warsaw than Treblinka was.
The Germans felt a little bit pressured because they probably had to speed up the gassing of the Jews, and they needed more gas chambers to accommodate this.
So in Majdanek, we were left out on a huge field.
I remember that our lips, our tongues were like leather.
I remember all the lips were so parched and crusty.
And we were so thirsty.
So they opened hoses and-- and-- and poured the water on the field so that we could lick the water from the ground.
And we did.
We saw on the field-- on the field, we saw on one side behind the barbed wires.
We saw the barbed wires.
I kind of see them on this-- on the left side, left of me.
There were children, just heaps of children who were just walking around.
You know.
Just children, because all the children were piled into one-- into one field.
And there were-- there were the children.
And then-- then-- should I pause?
Let's pause.
OK.
